methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 24.4x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
XMOS is a growth-stage semiconductor company that has raised £200M+ to date. The Chips & Semiconductors sector comprises 14 same-sector peers, including 3 public comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers such as Cerebras, PsiQuantum, Etched, SambaNova Systems, and Credo Semiconductor.

Traditional peer-median valuation does not apply here. The peer set lacks sufficient narrow-stage matches—only 1 adjacent-stage comparable and 0 same-stage peers met filtering criteria, falling short of the minimum 3 datapoints required for statistical reliability. Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples also cannot be applied: while Semiconductors trade at a median 24.4x P/S across the 3 public tickers, XMOS's revenue is not on file, as is typical for private companies. This precludes any revenue-based multiple application.

The analysis cannot triangulate to a specific valuation range given current data constraints. The most actionable takeaway is that revenue disclosure would unlock P/S-based anchoring to the 24.4x public multiple, though application to a private growth-stage company would require a discount adjustment. The primary uncertainty is revenue scale; secondary uncertainties include margin profile and capital intensity relative to peers. Further diligence should prioritize obtaining revenue figures and detailed peer transaction multiples from recent funding rounds in the private semiconductor universe.
